examples.title: DDA Line Drawing Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of Digital Differential Analyzer algorithm for drawing lines between two points
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DDA_Line(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 dx = x2 - x1
 dy = y2 - y1
 
 steps = MAX(ABS(dx), ABS(dy))
 
 xinc = dx / steps
 yinc = dy / steps
 
 x = x1
 y = y1
 
 FOR i = 0 to steps
 PUTPIXEL(ROUND(x), ROUND(y), color)
 x = x + xinc
 y = y + yinc
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm calculates the number of steps needed (max of absolute differences), computes incremental values, and plots pixels by adding these increments while rounding to integer coordinates.

examples.title: Bresenham's Line Drawing Algorithm
examples.description: Efficient integer-based line drawing algorithm for lines with slope between 0 and 1
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Bresenham_Line(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 dx = x2 - x1
 dy = y2 - y1
 
 P = 2 * dy - dx // Initial decision parameter
 
 x = x1
 y = y1
 
 PUTPIXEL(x, y, color)
 
 FOR x = x1+1 to x2
 IF P < 0 THEN
 // Choose East pixel
 P = P + 2 * dy
 ELSE
 // Choose North-East pixel
 y = y + 1
 P = P + 2 * dy - 2 * dx
 END IF
 PUTPIXEL(x, y, color)
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: Uses decision parameter P to choose between East (no y-increment) or North-East (y-increment) pixels. P is updated incrementally using only integer arithmetic.

examples.title: Bresenham's Algorithm - General Form
examples.description: Complete Bresenham algorithm handling all line orientations including steep slopes
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Bresenham_General(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 // Ensure we always iterate from left to right
 IF x1 > x2 THEN
 SWAP(x1, x2)
 SWAP(y1, y2)
 END IF
 
 dx = x2 - x1
 dy = y2 - y1
 
 // Determine octant and adjust
 IF ABS(dy) > ABS(dx) THEN
 // Line is steep (slope > 1 or < -1)
 // Swap roles of x and y
 RETURN Bresenham_Steep(y1, x1, y2, x2)
 ELSE
 RETURN Bresenham_Gentle(x1, y1, x2, y2)
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This general form handles all line orientations by detecting steep lines and swapping coordinates, then calling the appropriate specialized function.
